coredata.dc:title: The degradation of tetracycline by modified BiOCl nanosheets with carbon dots from the chlorella
coredata.dc:creator: Zha, Zhenxing
Lai, Jiahao
Li, Ying
Yang, Jing
Cui, Shihai
Li, Yafei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, chlorella was first used as the carbon source of Carbon dots (CDs) and further modified the BiOCl. Then, a novel photocatalyst Carbon dots/BiOCl (CBOC) was successfully synthesized. The morphology, crystal structure, chemical states and optical properties of the material were characterized by different techniques. Meanwhile, the energy band structure and density of states of CBOC2 composite were calculated based on density functional theory. In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the materials, the photodegradation of tetracycline was carried out under the simulated sunlight. The conditions of different materials, materials dosage and sample concentration were compared. About 99.5% tetracycline (10 mg/L) can be photodegraded by 15 mg CBOC2 in 75 min illumination with 52.2% mineralization rate. The material can be reused with high stability. The ·O2 − is the main reactive species. The possible degradation pathways and mechanism of photocatalysis was discussed in detail. The CDs originated from chlorella has the large contribution in the photocatalysis. The work creates a new road of the application of chlorella in photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots/BiOCl
Photocatalyst
Degradation
Tetracycline
Mechanism
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TC theoretical calculation and possible degradation pathway

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The detailed color photos of the materials. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The schematic crystal structure of chlorella (A); SEM images of BiOCl (B), BiOCl nanoplates (C) and CBOC2 (D); HRTEM images (E-F), elemental mapping (G) and EDS images (H) of CBOC2.
XPS spectra (A-E) of as-prepared materials and FT-IR spectra (F) of BiOCl (a), CBOC0.5 (b), CBOC1 (c), CBOC2 (d), CBOC4 (e).

Crystals of BiOCl (A) and CBOC2 (B) (The Bi, O, Cl and C atoms are represented by green, pink, blue and black spheres, respectively.); Band structures and the DOS of BiOCl (C, E) and CBOC2 (D, F); TDOS/PDOS of BiOCl (G) and CBOC2 (H). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The TC photocatalytic removal by different materials under simulated sunlight (A); The kinetics curves of CBOC2 (B); Effect of CBOC2 amount on TC removal rate (C); Effect of initial TC concentration on degradation under simulated sunlight (D).
The TC photocatalytic removal by different electrolytes (A) and pH (B) under simulated sunlight.
3D EEMs of the TC (A-D) solution after simulated sunlight irradiation durations of (0 min, 15 min, 45 min and 75 min).
The cycling experiments of CBOC2 (A); FT-IR spectra (B); The XRD analysis (C) and SEM images (D) of the CBOC2.
The table is Fukui index of TC (A); Optimized structure and for TC with DMol3/B3LYP (B); The HOMO (C) and LUMO (D) orbits of TC.
Possible degradation pathways of the TC molecules by the CBOC2 under simulated sunlight.
Schematic digram of photocatalysis by CBOC2 composite.

head.ce:title: The degradation of tetracycline by modified BiOCl nanosheets with carbon dots from the chlorella

Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, chlorella was first used as the carbon source of Carbon dots (CDs) and further modified the BiOCl. Then, a novel photocatalyst Carbon dots/BiOCl (CBOC) was successfully synthesized. The morphology, crystal structure, chemical states and optical properties of the material were characterized by different techniques. Meanwhile, the energy band structure and density of states of CBOC2 composite were calculated based on density functional theory. In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the materials, the photodegradation of tetracycline was carried out under the simulated sunlight. The conditions of different materials, materials dosage and sample concentration were compared. About 99.5% tetracycline (10 mg/L) can be photodegraded by 15 mg CBOC2 in 75 min illumination with 52.2% mineralization rate. The material can be reused with high stability. The ·O2 − is the main reactive species. The possible degradation pathways and mechanism of photocatalysis was discussed in detail. The CDs originated from chlorella has the large contribution in the photocatalysis. The work creates a new road of the application of chlorella in photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are often used to kill germs in human and animal bodies for the purpose of treating diseases, such as common skin diseases and organ infections. Tetracycline is a typical antibiotic medicine. It is usually added to pharmaceuticals and personal care products. However, antibiotics and metabolic intermediates were discovered in soil and surface water for the past a few years [1]. The concentration of tetracycline is about 50 mg/L in pig manure [2], which is much higher than the EU standard (10 mg/L) [3]. Studies show that they are difficult to decompose naturally due to the antibacterial properties and chemical stabilities. Therefore, some measures should be taken to make the antibiotics non-toxic or less toxic before releasing to the environment [4]. Nowadays, researchers have extensively explored the treatment methods, including physical adsorption, biodegradation, photocatalytic degradation or the combination of different ways [5–9]. Among these, photocatalysis is one of the environmentally friendly and sustainable strategies to eliminate the antibiotics wastewater pollution.

BiOCl is a semiconductor material with flake [Bi2O2]2+ layer. In recent years, it has been regarded as a common photocatalyst because of excellent light adsorption properties [10]. However, BiOCl has a typical wide band gap, so it is hard to absorb visible light [11–13]. In order to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of BiOCl, a lot of studies have been carried out to design and prepare BiOCl-based photocatalysts, including metal or nonmetal doping [14], semiconductor heterojunction and so on [15–17].

Non-metallic doping is very effective to enhance the performance of BiOCl [18]. Fluorine, bromine, iodine and carbon elements can usually improve the photocatalytic performance of BiOCl [19–22]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) doped BiOCl via solvothermal method is a novel method to prepare high efficiency photocatalyst CDs have the advantages of good dispersion [23], outstanding charge transport performance and easy functionalization, which can increase the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [24]. Feizpoor et al. [25], successfully synthesized CDs/TiO2/Ag6Si2O7 with high photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation. Tang et al. [26], synthesized CDs/m-BiVO4 material with different exposed crystal faces, improving the photodegradation efficiency of methyl blue under visible light irradiation.

Small organic molecules or oligomers are commonly used as carbon sources for CDs synthesis, usually including citric acid, glucose, polyethylene glycol, urea and ionic liquid [27]. However, they are all chemicals and cannot be facilely obtained from nature. In order to find the green and low-cost carbon source, natural products have been explored. Chlorella is widely distributed and easily bred on Earth. It plays the important role in the ecological balance [28]. However, large amount of chlorella can lead to red tides and environmental pollution [29]. In the lab, it is usually used as a target organism in environmental toxicity test because of the highly sensitivity to pollutants [30]. In this study, chlorella is first used as the carbon source of CDs and further modified the BiOCl. The preparation composite CDs/BiOCl not only effectively uses the redundant chlorella but also has the potential to solve the environmental problems aroused by excess antibiotics from the natural.

In this work, the binary composite CDs/BiOCl was synthesized via a easy method. The novel material was characterized by various analytical instruments. The crystal, morphology and optical performance of the material were studied [31]. Meanwhile, the band structure and density of states of the materials were calculated based on the density functional theory (DFT). The materials were used in the photocatalytic degradation of TC. The mechanism was explored.

The chemicals of this work have been listed in Supplementary Material (SM)-1. The chlorella was first washed alternately with water and EtOH. The clean chlorella was obtained via centrifugal separation. After that, it was dispersed into 40 mL solution (ethanol/ultrapure water: 3:1) with ultrasonication, then 0.485 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was put into the solution under intense magnetic stir. The 10 mL aqueous solution of KCl (0.0745 g) was injected to the mixture. Then they were all moved into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with 433 K for 12 h. After naturally cooling to the room temperature, the solid product CBOC was attained by centrifugation, cleaning and drying in turn. For comparison, the different materials with mass ratios of chlorella and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.5%, 1%, 2% and 4%) were synthesized and marked as CBOC0.5, CBOC1, CBOC2 and CBOC4. BiOCl was synthesized according to the recipe above without the addition of chlorella. The materials have been characterized by the instruments in SM-2. The detailed color photos of the materials were shown in Fig. 1 .

The photocatalytic process was carried out in a quartz tube-made-reactor with magnetic stir. The circulating cooling water made the reactor temperature stable at 25 ± 1 °C. A certain CBOC powder was put into the reactor with TC solution. After 30 min adsorption-desorption equilibrium, light source was offered by solar simulator furnished with 500 W Xenon lamp. At pre-set intervals of 0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75 min, 1 mL of solution was extracted with a pipette for analysis with UV–vis spectrometer at the maximum wavelength of 350 nm.

The morphologies of the materials were further investigated by SEM and TEM analysis. From SEM images, the freeze-dried chlorella has irregular shape with folded surface (Fig. 2 A). The BiOCl has a uniform tetragonal structure with high crystallinity (Fig. 2B) and the average side length is about 1 μm. Fig. 2C shows the crystal structure of BiOCl nanoplate. It is easy to observed that the BiOCl arranges a nicely lamellar structure into alternating layers of [Bi2O2]2+ and chlorine, which is consistent with that in Fig. 2B. For the composite of CBOC2, the sheet is obviously smaller than BiOCl (Fig. 2D). The average thickness is about 85 nm and the side length is about 0.5 μm. In the HRTEM image of CBOC2 (Fig. 2E), there are small black carbon dots (CDs) from the chlorella dispersed on the surface of the sheet with the diameter about 15 nm. The crystal lattices of 0.274 and 0.341 nm are ascribed to the (110) and (101) planes of the BiOCl, respectively (Fig. 2F). The elemental mapping images in Fig. 2G indicate the uniform distribution of four elements in the composite. Meanwhile, the EDS analysis (Fig. 2H) also confirms the coexistence of the four elements in CBOC2 composite. The elements weight percentages of Bi, O, Cl, and C is 70.58, 12.07, 10.19 and 7.16% (Fig. 2H), respectively.

Fig. S1 displays XRD patterns of the nanomaterials. The characteristic diffraction peaks (001), (002), (101), (110), (112), (200) and (212) in Fig. S1a are well consistent to the tetragonal crystal of BiOCl (JCPDS Card No. 06–0249), indicating the good crystallinity of the material via a solvothermal reaction. By comparison, the intensity of (001) peaks of materials progressively weakens as chlorella increases, implying that carbon dots inhibit the growth of the (001) crystal plane and interact with the BiOCl. Combined with the SEM analysis in Fig. 2D, the morphology of CBOC2 has a slight difference with BiOCl, while the crystal remains tetragonal BiOCl. It is connected with the inhibiting effect of carbon dots, which can be supported by Refs. [32]. In the enlarged pattern from 25.2 to 26.2°, the (002) crystal plane is found to transfer to higher angle [33]. It is corresponding to the participation of more carbon dots, implying the interaction of BiOCl and CDs.

The specific surface area has a significant effect on the performance of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. S1B, the adsorption-desorption isotherm curves of BiOCl and COBC2 were belong to classical Type IV, indicating the mesoporous feature of the materials. The SBET of BiOCl and COBC2 are 2.31 and 19.34 m2 g−1, respectively. It can be seen that CBOC2 can adsorption more pollutant molecules and has better photocatalytic performance.

In order to detect the structure, the BiOCl and CBOC2 were performed by XPS. Fig. 3 A is the full spectra of the two materials. It is obvious that Bi, O and Cl exist in the BiOCl. Besides, there is a bit carbon from air. In Fig. 3B, there are two peaks at 159.6 and 164.9 eV. They can be indexed to the characteristic peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 [34]. The CBOC2 has two more peaks at 160.4 and 165.6 eV, which is connected with the Bi2+ produced by the reduction of partial Bi3+ by carbon [35]. In Fig. 3C, the O 1s region of CBOC2 is classified into three peaks. Among them, the peak at 530.0 eV can be attributed to the lattice oxygen in the Bi–O bonds, which is weaker than that of BiOCl. The peak at 531.6 eV ascribes to oxygen vacancies, which is stronger than that of BiOCl. Another peak at 533.4 eV is associated with hydroxide oxygen (OH−) [36,37] from the surface water of the materials. The Cl 2p spectra of CBOC2 can be divided into three peaks at 197.8, 199.2 and 200.6 eV, which are assigned to the binding energies of Cl 2p3/2, Cl 2p1/2 and C–Cl, respectively (Fig. 3D) [38]. The C 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 3E with three main peaks. The strong peak at 284.8 eV is assigned to C–O [39]. The peaks at 286.0 eV and 288.8 eV belong to C=O and C–Cl, respectively.

The important chemical bonds and functional groups of BiOCl and CBOC composites were identified by FT-IR (Fig. 3F). In regard to BiOCl, the peak at around 527 cm−1 belongs to Bi–O [40]. This means the successful synthesis of BiOCl. The characteristic peaks at 3448 cm−1 are from materials surface-adsorbed water. In the CBOC, the peaks around 1020, 1405 and 1420 cm−1 belong to typical vibrations of C–O and C=O, respectively [41]. Additionally, the small absorption peaks in CBOC2 at 762 cm−1 may correspond to C–Cl [42], which is consistent with the result of XPS analysis. The carbon containing groups come from the high temperature carbonization of chlorella.

Fig. S2A displays the UV–vis DRS of BiOCl and CBOC2. The adsorption wavelength of the BiOCl is below 360 nm, which indicates that the material has a response in the ultraviolet region. The adsorption of CBOC2 has an obvious red-shift, so the nanocomposite has a UV–visible spectral response. From the right-top inset of Fig. S2A, the band gaps (Eg) of the BiOCl and CBOC2 are 3.31 and 2.87 eV, respectively. The Eg is estimated from the plots of (αhν)1/2 versus photo energy. The EVB and ECB are calculated as below.
(1)
ECB = X - Ee - 1 / 2 Eg
(2)
EVB = ECB + Eg
where Ee (about 4.50 eV) is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale; X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor. After calculation, the CB and VB are 0.49 eV and 3.80 eV for BiOCl, while 0.34 eV and 3.14 eV for CBOC2, respectively.

PL spectra of BiOCl and CBOC2 are displayed in Fig. S2B. The BiOCl has stronger emissions centered at about 420 nm, indicating the quick recombination of e− and h+ [43]. The weaker emissions of CBOC2 suggest that the recombination rate is effectively inhibited because of the existence of CDs [44].

In order to verify the charge separation of BiOCl and CBOC2, photocurrent (Fig. S2C) and EIS (Fig. S2D) analysis were conducted. Obviously, the two materials demonstrate stable and invertible photocurrents. The CBOC2 photocurrent is around 2.5 times higher than that of BiOCl, which indicate that CBOC2 has decreased recombination rate of photo-induced carriers. For EIS Nyquist plot, smaller arc is observed in CBOC2, which means the charge transfer resistance declines and the electron transfer rate enhances [45]. The result is corresponding to the PL and photocurrent test.

The electronic structures of BiOCl and CBOC2 materials were calculated by Materials Studio (MS) software. According to the initial unit construction confirmed by HRTEM in Fig. 2 and XRD in Fig. S1, the optimized structure of BiOCl (space group No. 129; D7-4 h) and CBOC2 composite are provided in Fig. 4 (A-B). Obviously, the lattice type of the materials is tetragonal. The band structures are shown in Fig. 4C and D). The correlative total DOS (TDOS) and the project DOS (PDOS) in Fig. 4G is for BiOCl and 6H for CBOC2. They are according to the DOS diagrams in Fig. 4E and F, respectively. All calculations were performed by using CASTEP operation in the software based on DFT. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the Eg of the materials are 3.25 eV, 2.78 eV, respectively. They are consistent of the experimental results in Fig. S2A. The valence band top (VBT, located at R point) and conduction band bottom (CBB, located at G point) of BiOCl are observed in Fig. 4C. It is shown that the BiOCl is an indirect band-gap semiconductor. However, the VBT and CBB of CBOC2 are situated at M point, which means the direct band-gap semiconductor [46,47]. Generally, when the curves of band structures are more intensive, the strength of the peaks in DOS diagram are larger and there are more electrons [48,49]. By comparison, CBOC2 has more intensive band structure due to the carbon doping, which indicates more electrons internally. The electrons can contribute to more photo-induced carriers and increase the photocatalytic effect. The TDOS/PDOS in Fig. 4G and H can explain the orbital contributions of electrons. Evidently, VBT of BiOCl comprises Bi 6p, O 2p and Cl 2p orbitals, whereas the CBB is composed of Bi 6p, and O 2p orbitals [50]. As for the CBOC2 composite (Fig. 4H), the VBT is mostly occupied by Bi 6p, Cl 2p and C 2p orbitals, while the CBB is the combination of Bi 6p and C 2p orbitals [51]. Thus, the VBT of the CBOC2 composite has upshifted and the Eg of the composite will be narrowed, owing to the orbital hybridization between Bi 6p and C 2p states [52,53]. The narrowed Eg is beneficial for the migration of photo-generated electrons to the reaction sites, thereby effectively enhance the separation of photo-induced carriers and accelerate photocatalytic degradation of TC. Calculation method above has been displayed in SM-3.

The photocatalytic performance is assessed by TC degradation by different materials under simulated sunlight illumination. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, the TC concentration has nearly no decrease in light without the catalyst. After 75 min illumination, about 58.5% of TC is degraded by the BiOCl, whereas 99.5% of TC is degraded by the CBOC2. The degradation efficiency of TC first increases then decreases as the amount of chlorella differs from 0.5% to 4.0%. It is indicated that the appropriate decoration of the BiOCl is conductive to enhance the photocatalytic activity. However, the overdose of CDs on the surface may suppress the light reception of BiOCl. Similar studies have been verified in Ref. [54]. The reaction rate by different materials can be represented by pseudo first-order equation:
(3)
In
( C0/Ct )
= kt
where k is the rate constant; C0 and Ct are the TC concentrations at 0 and t min, respectively. In Fig. 5B, the k of the CBOC2 is (0.075 min−1), which is about 3 times that of BiOCl (0.0242 min−1). In Fig. 5C, when the dosage of CBOC2 is 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 mg, the TC removal rate is about 92.5%, 95.5%, 97.0%, 98.0% and 99.3% after 75 min, respectively. From Fig. 5D, the initial TC concentration has an important effect on the photocatalysis. The photocatalytic efficiency is 99.3%, 75.3%, 46.8%, 32.1% and 21.7% as the TC concentration increased. The pollutants with high concentration may hinder the light transmission and photon energy. In order to further explore the application of the material, the degradation of RhB was carried out and the removal efficiency reaches 99.5% within 25 min by COBC2 (Fig. S3). It can be proved that the material COBC2 has the broad application in not only the antibiotic (TC) but also the dye (RhB).

As far as we know, environment water often contains Cl−, SO4 −2 and CO3 −2 ions. In order to evaluate the effect of the above ions on the photocatalytic degradation effect of TC, NaCl, Na2CO3 and Na2SO4 are introduced in the photocatalytic experiment. It can be seen from Fig. 6 A that the degradation efficiency of TC decreases to varying degrees in different electrolytes with a concentration of 1 mol/L. After adding Na2CO3, the degradation efficiency dropped from 99.3% to 62.7%, which is mainly because CO3 −2 can act as free radical masking agents [55]. It is worth noting that the addition of NaCl and Na2SO4 also slightly reduces the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC, the competitive adsorption between Cl− (or SO4 −2) and TC may be the reason of the decrease in photocatalytic efficiency.

The pH is an important factor affecting the formation of active species in the photocatalytic process. In Fig. 6B, when the pH is neutral, CBOC2 has the highest photocatalytic efficiency for TC. But as the pH value increases or decreases, the photodegradation efficiency is obviously inhibited. Especially when the pH is 3 and 11, the degradation efficiency of TC is 53.7% and 44.8%, respectively. The reason may be that the catalyst is partially dissolved under the peracid condition, which leads to a loss of the catalyst dosage [56]. At the same time, under high pH conditions, the adsorption efficiency will increase, so that excess TC will be adsorbed on the surface of CBOC2 and reduce the light absorption rate, which is not conducive to the production of e− and h+.

For the purpose of testing the mineralization rate of pollutants in the process of photocatalytic degradation, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was performed. Fig. S4 displays the removal efficiency of the target analytes by different catalyst with and without simulated sunlight after 75 min irradiation. The CBOC2 exhibites the maximum mineralization rate of 52.2%, which is better than that of chlorella (9.5%) or BiOCl (21.9%).

The 3D-EEM was carried out to anlysis photodegradation process of TC (Fig. 7 A–D) solution. The fluorescence peaks of TC at Ex/Em = 275/425 nm. As displayed in Fig. 7A, it can be seen that a weak peak at 0 min. As the time of exposure to visible light increases, the intensity of peak gradually decreases. After 75 min irradiation (Fig. 7D), the peak signal was disappeared, which means that TC was degraded into intermediate products.

In order to assess the stability of the CBOC2, cycling experiments were performed. For each cycle, the photocatalyst was collected and reused after centrifugation, washing and drying. As depicted in Fig. 8 A, the photocatalytic activity of CBOC2 has little deactivation after five successive recycles. The degradation efficiency decreases by 8.2%. Furthermore, the newly synthesized and used materials have been compared. The vibration peaks in Fig. 8B and the diffraction peaks in Fig. 8C have no obvious differences [57,58]. Compared with Fig. 2D, the morphology of the CBOC2 material has not changed significantly (Fig. 8D). The structure and the crystalline phase of the CBOC2 cannot be changed evidently by the light irradiation. The results reveal the excellent stability of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC theoretical calculation and possible degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of TC optimized through DFT theoretical calculation. Calculated Cartesian coordinates (Table S1) and explored the photocatalytic degradation path of TC. The HOMO orbital of TC is mainly located on the benzene ring, while the LUMO orbital of TC is mainly located on the hydrocarbon group (Fig. 9 C and D). Fig. 9A depicts TC Fukui index. C6 sites are easy to be attacked by ·OH radicals to form hydroxylated products, while C16 and C18 sites tend to accept ·H, ·O2 − and h+ radicals, leading to ring-opening reaction. Possible degradation pathways of the TC molecules by the CBOC2 under simulated sunlight (Fig. 10 ).

Generally, h+, ·O2 − and ·OH are the main active species in the photocatalytic experiment. To explore degradation mechanism, different scavengers were added in the reactor, including SO (h+), BQ (·O2 −) and IPA (·OH). In Fig. S5, the removal percentage is 99.5% without the scavenger. After the addition of IPA, the degradation efficiency has a slight decrease to 88.8%, showing a small impact of ·OH. It is worth noting that the removal efficiency declines dramatically to 15.3% or 19.6% by adding BQ or SO, indicating the ·O2 − and h+ are the primary active species.

To extensively investigate the presence of active radicals in the process, the ESR spin trap technique was performed. As depicted in Fig. 11 , no obvious DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH peaks can be found in darkness. However, after exposed to simulated sunlight 2 min, the DMPO-·O2 − (1:1:1:1) are checked and the signals enhance, manifesting the ·O2 − radicals are generated (Fig. 11A). The DMPO-·OH (1:2:2:1) are observed although they are not strong, implying that the ·OH radicals also exist in CBOC2 photocatalytic process (Fig. 11B).

Based on the results above, the photocatalysis mechanism can be further elaborated below. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, BiOCl possesses broad Eg of 3.31 eV and weak visible light absorption (Fig. S2A). The photo-excited electrons migrate from the VB to the CB of the material, remaining holes on the VB. Most of them recombine quickly in the BiOCl and the photocatalytic efficiency is relatively low (Fig. 5A). Meanwhile, the CB of BiOCl (0.49 eV) is positive than the Eө of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE), which is not conducive to the generation of ·O2 − radicals [59]. When the BiOCl was modified by chlorella, the carbon dots generate and the oxygen in BiOCl lattice is partially replaced by carbon. The composite CBOC2 has the narrower Eg of 2.87 eV and stronger light absorption than BiOCl. Thus the photoresponse expands from UV to visible light. More photons can be absorbed and more photoinduced carriers generate. The photogenerated electrons can be easily moved to CDs and enhance the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, which is supported by the analysis in Fig. 4C-F. The CDs possess super electron transport capability, and promote the formation of more superoxide radicals [60–62]. The results correspond to the trapping experiments in Fig. S4 and ESR analysis in Fig. 11. Afterwards, ·O2 − with strong oxidizing property can combine H+ to generate H2O2 (the Eө of O2/H2O2 is 0.682 eV vs. NHE) and further ·OH (the Eө of ·OH/H2O is 2.72 eV vs. NHE). The active radicals and h+ on the VB of the CBOC2 can oxidize the target contaminants TC. Thus the photodegradation achieves with high efficiency. The simulated diagram for possible photocatalytic mechanism is displayed in Fig. 12 .

In summary, the novel CDs/BiOCl nanosheets were prepared through typical solvothermal method. The chemical and physical properties of the photocatalyst were characterized by TEM, XRD, UV–vis DRS, FT-IR and XPS, respectively. The CBOC2 composite demonstrate higher photocatalytic performance than BiOCl and other materials (CBOC0.5, CBOC1 and CBOC4) under the simulated sunlight. The degradation efficiency of TC by CBOC2 is about 3 times than that of BiOCl. The CDs originated from chlorella has the following two aspects: (1) the oxygen in BiOCl lattice is partially replaced by carbon, then the Eg narrowed and the light response expands from ultraviolet light to visible light. (2) CDs have remarkable electron acceptance and migration ability, which can enhance the charge separation efficiency of the CBOC2. Furthermore, DFT calculation shows CBOC2 is a direct semiconductor with intensive band structure, strong peaks in DOS diagram and more internal electrons, which contribute to more photo-induced carriers. The scavenger experiments and ESR spectra indicate that ·O2 − is the main active species in photocatalysis process. Based on the researches the photocatalytic mechanism has been explored. This work provides potential application of biological materials modified semiconductor materials.
There were no human or animals test participants involved in this study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare that there was no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21177061), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20150968) and Technology-Based Pioneering Enterprise Incubation Project of Jiangsu Province (BC2016003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.157454.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, H. Zhong, L. Fan, S. Yang, 53% efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light emitting diodes. Adv. Mater. 29 (2017) 1702910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanotubes: excellent charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, X. Liu, J. Liu, B. Han, X. Hu, F. Yang, Z. Xu, Y. Li, S. Jia, Z. Li, Y. Zhao,. Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanotubes: excellent charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57 (2018) 5765-5771.
